lessons.title: Evaluating Sources
lessons.description: Learn Evaluating Sources with focused discussion, examples, activity, and a 5-item quiz.
objectives: Understand key ideas in Evaluating Sources
Analyze topic-specific examples
Apply learning through the activity
Answer quiz items with evidence-based reasoning

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: This lesson explores Evaluating Sources for Grade 10 English learners.

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Evaluating sources means judging whether information is reliable, accurate, current, and useful for a purpose. Not every website, article, video, or post is equally trustworthy. A good source has clear authorship, evidence, and a purpose that can be examined.

To evaluate a source, check the author, date, evidence, publication, and possible bias. For example, a recent article from a recognized science organization is usually stronger for a research paper than an anonymous social media post with no evidence.

Evaluating sources matters because students use information to make claims and decisions. This skill helps you avoid misinformation, support research writing, and choose evidence that readers can trust.

audioNarration.transcript: Narration for Evaluating Sources: Evaluating sources means judging whether information is reliable, accurate, current, and useful for a purpose. Not every website, article, video, or post is equally trustworthy. A good source has clear authorship, evidence, and a purpose that can be examined.

sections.heading: Visual/Animated Explanation
sections.content: Visual guide for Evaluating Sources.

sections.heading: Guided Examples
items: Identify the main idea in a text about Evaluating Sources.
Pick the best supporting details.
Write an inference with textual evidence.

terms.meaning: Central thought of a text.
Information that explains the main idea.
Conclusion based on clues.
Writer attitude toward topic.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: This lesson on Evaluating Sources covered the key ideas, examples, activity, and quiz feedback.

activity.instruction: Match each source evaluation check with its purpose in reading and research writing.

items.answer: Shows whether the information is current.
Shows support for the source claims.
Shows possible unfair influence or one-sided purpose.

quiz.question: What does evaluating sources mean?
choices: Judging whether information is reliable, accurate, current, and useful
Accepting every source automatically
Only reading the title
Ignoring evidence
quiz.answer: Judging whether information is reliable, accurate, current, and useful
quiz.explanation: Source evaluation checks trustworthiness and usefulness.

quiz.question: Why check the author?
choices: To know who created the information
To remove the source
To avoid reading
To change the date
quiz.answer: To know who created the information
quiz.explanation: Authorship helps judge credibility.

quiz.question: Why check the date?
choices: To know whether information is current
To count paragraphs
To identify nouns
To avoid evidence
quiz.answer: To know whether information is current
quiz.explanation: Some topics need recent information.

quiz.question: Which source is stronger for a science research paper?
choices: A recent article from a recognized science organization
An anonymous post with no evidence
A rumor from a comment section
A source with no author and no date
quiz.answer: A recent article from a recognized science organization
quiz.explanation: It has stronger credibility and evidence.

quiz.question: Why evaluate sources?
choices: To avoid misinformation and support research writing
To use weak claims
To remove all sources
To ignore bias
quiz.answer: To avoid misinformation and support research writing
quiz.explanation: Evaluation helps students choose trustworthy evidence.

lessons.title: Research Writing
lessons.description: Learn Research Writing with focused discussion, examples, activity, and a 5-item quiz.
objectives: Understand key ideas in Research Writing
Analyze topic-specific examples
Apply learning through the activity
Answer quiz items with evidence-based reasoning

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: This lesson explores Research Writing for Grade 10 English learners.

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Research writing is writing that explains a topic using information from reliable sources. It begins with a focused question or topic, then uses evidence from books, articles, studies, interviews, or credible websites. The writer must organize the information clearly and give credit to sources.

Good research writing does not simply copy facts. It explains, connects, and interprets evidence. For example, a paragraph may introduce an idea, cite evidence from a source, then explain how that evidence supports the main point.

Research writing matters because it teaches students to investigate carefully and communicate responsibly. It is used in academic papers, reports, proposals, and many careers where decisions depend on trustworthy information.

audioNarration.transcript: Narration for Research Writing: Research writing is writing that explains a topic using information from reliable sources. It begins with a focused question or topic, then uses evidence from books, articles, studies, interviews, or credible websites. The writer must organize the information clearly and give credit to sources.

sections.heading: Visual/Animated Explanation
sections.content: Visual guide for Research Writing.

sections.heading: Guided Examples
items: Identify the main idea in a text about Research Writing.
Pick the best supporting details.
Write an inference with textual evidence.

terms.meaning: Central thought of a text.
Information that explains the main idea.
Conclusion based on clues.
Writer attitude toward topic.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: This lesson on Research Writing covered the key ideas, examples, activity, and quiz feedback.

activity.instruction: Arrange the learning flow for Research Writing.
items: Identify how explains is defined in Research Writing.
Describe the key topic step taught in the discussion.
Apply information in a concrete situation from the lesson.
Explain the reliable based reason why Research Writing matters.
correctOrder: Identify how explains is defined in Research Writing.
Describe the key topic step taught in the discussion.
Apply information in a concrete situation from the lesson.
Explain the reliable based reason why Research Writing matters.

quiz.question: What is research writing?
choices: Writing that explains a topic using reliable sources
Writing only personal guesses
Writing without evidence
Copying facts without thinking
quiz.answer: Writing that explains a topic using reliable sources
quiz.explanation: Research writing depends on credible information.

quiz.question: What should guide the start of research?
choices: A focused question or topic
A random answer
A copied conclusion
A font choice only
quiz.answer: A focused question or topic
quiz.explanation: A focused question directs the investigation.

quiz.question: Why give credit to sources?
choices: To show where borrowed information came from
To hide the evidence
To avoid responsibility
To remove the source
quiz.answer: To show where borrowed information came from
quiz.explanation: Credit makes research responsible and traceable.

quiz.question: What should a research paragraph do after citing evidence?
choices: Explain how the evidence supports the point
Stop without explanation
Change to an unrelated topic
Delete the main idea
quiz.answer: Explain how the evidence supports the point
quiz.explanation: Research writing connects evidence to the writer meaning.

quiz.question: True or False: explains is a key lesson concept in Research Writing.
quiz.answer: True
quiz.explanation: The discussion identifies explains as part of the lesson understanding.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: This lesson explores Synthesis of Sources for Grade 10 English.

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Synthesis of Sources is an important topic because it builds a clear definition of the lesson ideas and connects them to academic tasks. In this discussion, students learn how Synthesis of Sources works and why it appears in real classroom and community situations.

A strong understanding of Synthesis of Sources requires explicit strategy and careful use of terms. For example, a focused example shows how the concept is applied, how reasoning is checked, and how mistakes can be corrected with evidence.

Synthesis of Sources matters because it strengthens importance and helps students make justified decisions. This lesson also reinforces reading based reasoning so learners can explain answers instead of relying on guessing.
